sanskrit_devanagari:
यः सर्वत्रानभिस्नेहस्तत्तत्प्राप्य शुभाशुभम् ।
नाभिनन्दति न द्वेष्टि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ५७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yaḥ sarvatrānabhisnehas tat tat prāpya śubhāśubham |
nābhinandati na dveṣṭi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā || 57 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses simplified IAST without diacritics)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.57।। rather than ॥ ५७ ॥)
IAST exact (body text matches under standard romanization; raw_capture_path: data/sources/raw/bg-2-57-bgus.json)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.057 = MBh_06,024.057). Body text identical: 'yaḥ sarvatrānabhisnehastattatprāpya śubhāśubham | nābhinandati na dveṣṭi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā'. GRETIL writes 'sarvatrānabhisnehas tat tat' as run-together compound; modern editions split. Scribal-convention only.

anvaya.meaning: and that (the doubling 'tat tat' = 'this and that, whatever it may be')

translations.verbatim_quote: In the material world, one who is unaffected by whatever good or evil he may obtain, neither praising it nor despising it, is firmly fixed in perfect knowledge.
One who remains unattached under all conditions, and is neither delighted by good fortune nor dejected by tribulation, he is a sage with perfect knowledge.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There is always some upheaval in the material world which may be good or evil. One who is not agitated by such material upheavals, who is unaffected by good and evil, is to be understood to be fixed in Kṛṣṇa consciousness.

Further, prajñā, the wisdom; tasya, of that person, of that sannyāsin; pratiṣṭhitā, remains established; yaḥ, who; anabhisnehaḥ, has no attachment for; sarvatra, anything anywhere, even for body, life, etc.; who na abhinandati, neither welcomes; na dveṣṭi, nor rejects; tat tat, anything whatever; śubha-aśubham, good or bad; prāpya, when he comes across it. Of such a person, who is thus free from elation or dejection, the wisdom arising from discrimination remains established.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads sarvatra anabhisneha as universal non-attachment — the sannyāsin has no special attachment 'anywhere', not even to body or life. Encountering good or bad, he neither welcomes (abhinandati) nor rejects (dveṣṭi). The Advaita gloss anchors the freedom in discriminative wisdom: viveka between Self and not-Self produces the steadiness.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: He, who has no love for all pleasing objects, i.e., who is indifferent to them, and who does not feel attraction or repulsion when he is united with or separated from attractive or repulsive objects respectively, who neither rejoices at the former, nor hates the latter — he also is of firm wisdom. Sri Krsna now mentions the next lower state.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja sets up a hierarchy across 2.55-2.58: each verse describes a successively lower state below the highest. 2.55 is the highest (jñāna-bhakti); 2.56 is the next; 2.57 is one step below — the sage who, on encountering good or bad, neither rejoices nor hates. Vishishtadvaita keeps the gradation explicit; the verse names a real psychological achievement, not just an absence.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Lord Krishna explains here that one devoid of attachment to everything, when exposed to that which is good does not rejoice, neither does such a one lament when exposed to that which is not good.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Madhva renders the verse compactly: detached from everything, on encountering good neither rejoices nor on encountering evil laments. The Dvaita reading does not impose a metaphysical reduction; the verse is read as a behavioural marker.

positions.rendering: no attachment for anything anywhere, even for body, life, etc.
no love for all pleasing objects; indifferent to them

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Sanskrit 'sarvatra anabhisneha' is plain — 'unattached everywhere'. Shankara extends the scope to include body and life themselves (a strict Advaita reading: the sannyāsin's non-attachment is total). Ramanuja narrows to 'pleasing objects' (a Vishishtadvaita move that preserves devotion to the Supreme as outside the scope of non-attachment). Prabhupada introduces 'in the material world' (a Gaudiya scoping that separates the material from the devotional). Mukundananda stays close to the literal. The verse text itself is universal — sarvatra = everywhere — and any narrowing is commentarial.

positions.rendering: whatever good or evil he may obtain
tat tat, anything whatever; śubha-aśubham, good or bad; prāpya, when he comes across it

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The doubled 'tat tat' is an emphatic distributive — 'this and that, whatever it may be'. śubhāśubham is the dvandva 'good-and-bad'. The verse is naming the operator's response under either kind of input, not just under bad. The non-rejoicing in good is as load-bearing as the non-hating in bad — both are explicitly named.

literal_meaning: Whoever is unattached everywhere, and on attaining this or that good or bad, neither rejoices nor hates — their wisdom is established.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse names a behavioural mark of the sthita-prajña: under any input — good or bad, fortune or tribulation — the response shape is the same. The sage neither rejoices (na abhinandati) at the good nor hates (na dveṣṭi) the bad. Importantly, both halves are explicit: the non-rejoicing is named first, before the non-hating. This is the same equanimity-doctrine prefigured at 2.38 (sukha-duḥkhe same kṛtvā — make pleasure-and-pain equal) and at 2.48 (samatvaṁ yoga ucyate — equanimity is yoga); 2.57 specifies it for the realized one. Shankara reads the non-attachment as universal (extending to body and life); Ramanuja reads it as a step in a hierarchy of states (one below 2.55-2.56); Madhva reads it as a plain behavioural marker. All three agree on the structural point: the operator's response shape is invariant across the polarity of inputs.
